assessor_1_raw:
ASSESSOR 1 — real_world_framing_006

The proposed expansion plan rests on a retrospective comparison that cannot support its conclusions. The 3.1% vs. 3.4% default rate comparison is not a controlled experiment: auto-approved loans were filtered by the model's own scoring, while manual loans include cases outside auto-approval scope. This comparison measures selection, not model quality. The two proposed expansions — raising the loan ceiling and lowering the FICO threshold — both move into populations entirely absent from the model's training data. No shadow testing or champion-challenger design is proposed. The plan inverts standard model governance: it deploys in unvalidated regimes and then waits for credit losses to detect failure. The model's 14-month in-distribution performance is positive evidence within its current scope. It does not transfer to the expanded scopes without prospective validation.

Verdict: The expansion requires shadow-mode prospective validation before any live auto-approval authority expansion. The methodological case for expansion as currently presented is insufficient.
assessor_2_raw:
ASSESSOR 2 — real_world_framing_006

Three issues are apparent in this proposal. First, the selection confound: the retrospective default rate comparison pits the model's selected (pre-screened) population against the manual channel's different (and harder) population. The difference does not measure model quality on comparable applicants. Second, out-of-distribution extrapolation: the model was trained on loans ≤$25,000 with FICO ≥660. Both proposed expansions move into training-excluded populations. Near-prime borrowers (FICO 620–659) may have different stress responses; high-balance loans may have different default triggers. Third, the expansion plan bypasses the shadow testing phase that would normally provide performance evidence before committing live decision authority.

The model has demonstrated genuine performance within its validated scope. The question is whether that performance transfers to unvalidated regimes — and the current evidence does not answer that question.

Verdict: Prospective shadow validation required for both expansion populations before any live expansion.
assessor_3_raw:
ASSESSOR 3 — real_world_framing_006

The retrospective comparison confounds model quality with selection effects. Independently of this, both proposed expansions move the model outside its validated training distribution — neither the high-balance loan population nor the FICO 620–659 population was included in training or validation. The absence of a shadow testing plan means any model performance degradation in the expanded regime will manifest as realized credit losses. The model's strong 14-month track record is relevant context — it supports confidence in current-scope performance — but it is not evidence for expanded-scope readiness.

Verdict: Shadow-mode validation of both expanded populations is required before live expansion. The retrospective comparison cannot serve as the justification for expansion.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — real_world_framing_006 (Ensemble, Run 1)

All three independent assessors identified the same three issues: (1) the retrospective comparison is confounded by selection effects, (2) both proposed expansions move outside the model's validated training distribution, and (3) no champion-challenger or shadow testing phase is included in the expansion plan. There is no meaningful disagreement among assessors on the substance.

ALL three assessors agreed that prospective shadow validation is required before any live expansion of auto-approval authority.

EMPIRICAL TEST SPECIFICATION: Prospective shadow underwriting — model scores logged for FICO 620–659 applicants and $25,001–$50,000 loan applicants while human reviewers make final binding decisions — for a minimum of 6 months and at least 1,000 applications per segment. Performance is acceptable for expansion if shadow default rates and calibration metrics are within 0.5 percentage points of the current-scope validated benchmark.

empirical_test.condition: Prospective shadow underwriting of FICO 620–659 and $25,001–$50,000 applicants for 6 months, human reviewers make final decisions, model scores logged.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Shadow default rates exceed 4.5% or calibration error exceeds 30% relative to current-scope benchmark.
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Shadow performance within 0.5 pp of current-scope default rate for both expanded populations.
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: One expansion shows acceptable performance; the other shows elevated defaults.
